messages.content:
You are a GDPR compliance analyst. When given a document excerpt, evaluate it step by step against each GDPR requirement. For each step, quote the relevant text if found, or state it is missing. End with a structured verdict listing all violations. Always follow this exact format:

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): ...
Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): ...
Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): ...
Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): ...
Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): ...
Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): ...
Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): ...
Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): ...
Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): ...
Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): ...

VERDICT: [Compliant / Non-Compliant / Partially Compliant]
VIOLATIONS: [list each article breached]
RECOMMENDATIONS: [one line per fix needed]

Assess the following recruitment privacy notice for GDPR compliance:

"When you apply for a job at TalentCo, we collect your CV, cover letter, and contact details. We process this information to evaluate your suitability for the role. If unsuccessful, we may retain your details for up to 6 months to consider you for future roles, unless you ask us to delete them sooner. We do not share your data with third parties. You can request a copy of your data or ask us to delete it by emailing hr@talentco.com."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PARTIAL] Implied but not stated. Processing for recruitment evaluation implies Art. 6(1)(b) (steps prior to contract) or Art. 6(1)(f) (legitimate interest for talent pooling). Neither is explicitly named.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PARTIAL] Not addressed. CVs frequently contain special category data (disabilities, health conditions, nationality implying ethnicity). No policy for handling or avoiding such data is stated.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Two purposes stated and separated: evaluating the current application and considering for future roles. The second purpose is opt-out rather than opt-in, which is acceptable under legitimate interest but should be clearly framed.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PASS] CV, cover letter, and contact details are standard and proportionate for recruitment.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] "Up to 6 months" for unsuccessful candidates with an early deletion option is reasonable and transparent.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PARTIAL] Rights to access (Art. 15) and erasure (Art. 17) are mentioned. Rights to rectification (Art. 16), portability (Art. 20), restriction (Art. 18), and objection (Art. 21) are absent.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PASS] "We do not share your data with third parties" is a clear and compliant statement. Note: ATS software providers technically qualify as processors — if used, they should be disclosed.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [PASS] Not raised as an issue given no third-party sharing stated.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [FAIL] Not mentioned. An HR contact is provided but no DPO disclosed.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [FAIL] Not addressed.

VERDICT: Partially Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 6 (lawful basis not explicitly named), Art. 9 (special category data risk in CVs not addressed), Art. 15-21 (incomplete rights)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Explicitly name the lawful basis for each processing purpose
- Add a note advising applicants not to include special category data, and state how it will be handled if received
- Add rectification, portability, restriction, and objection rights
- Disclose any ATS or HR software providers as processors
- Confirm DPO requirement and disclose contact if applicable
